name: Tribute Eyebrow
flavor_profile: A robust, full-bodied white tea with noticeable sweetness. The syrupy brew carries nectar and cinnamon notes, with more depth and darker character than higher-grade whites. Seed-grown bushes give it added complexity and a rounder, thicker texture in the cup.
tasting_notes: Gong Mei is the honest one in the Fujian white lineup — less pretty than the bud grades, cheaper, and frankly more interesting once you stop ranking teas by price. The seed-grown bushes throw out a thicker liquor than factory clonal whites, and the cup has a cinnamon-and-nectar register you don't quite get in Bai Mu Dan. Boiling water is fine. In fact it's better — anything cooler and the tea reads thin, because the leaves are coarse and need heat to open up. First steep fast, five to ten seconds, then build. By the third infusion there's a brown-sugar thickness that sits on the tongue. Where it falls down: cheaper Gong Mei can taste papery or generically sweet, like hay water with a caramel edge. Look for a brew that's actually syrupy in the mouth, not just sweet in flavor. Aged versions deepen into dried fruit and wet wood.

brewing.tips: Can handle boiling water, especially aged versions. The larger leaves extract quickly so keep early steeps short. Aged Gong Mei benefits from a brief rinse to wake the leaves.
